definition_short: A small, partially enclosed spot offers privacy or seclusion within a larger space.
definition_long: Use when there is a clearly defined niche, alcove, or recess that provides a sense of privacy or enclosure. The niche should feel separated from primary circulation or open exposure. It can be created by walls, screens, furniture wings, or architectural recesses. Apply even if no person is present. Do not use for open seating without enclosing boundaries.
examples_positive: A window alcove with side walls and a built-in bench.
A booth recessed into a wall with high side panels.
examples_negative: Open bench seating along a main corridor.
A large open lounge with no enclosure.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for small regions bounded on at least two sides with a visible opening to the main space. Enclosure cues include side walls, screens, or high-backed seating. The niche should appear secondary to the main area and not a full room. Avoid tagging when the area is fully exposed.
extraction_notes_3d: Use proximity to surrounding surfaces to compute local enclosure or refuge scores. Tag when a sub-area has high enclosure and low exposure relative to the main space. Use ray casting to confirm limited sightlines into the niche from main circulation.

definition_short: An open area is highly exposed and lacks enclosure or shielding from views.
definition_long: Use when a space reads as open, exposed, and lacking privacy, such as a central plaza or wide open lobby. The key cue is low enclosure and high visibility from multiple directions. Apply to areas intended for public gathering or passage with minimal visual protection. Do not use for semi-enclosed or screened spaces. This tag contrasts with private/enclosed niches.
examples_positive: A central atrium floor with no partitions or screens.
A large open concourse visible from multiple levels.
examples_negative: A seating area with screens and partial walls.
A recessed lounge tucked behind columns.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for large contiguous open floor area with minimal boundary elements. High visibility across the space and lack of nearby shielding are strong cues. Use perspective to estimate how many directions the area is exposed to. Avoid confusing openness with emptiness if there is clear enclosure.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute isovist area or visibility graph for the zone. Tag when local enclosure is low and visibility from multiple directions is high. Use distance to nearest occluding surfaces to measure exposure.

definition_short: A clear pattern repeats across surfaces or elements in a regular, consistent way.
definition_long: Use when a visible pattern or motif repeats at a consistent scale, spacing, or rhythm across a surface or array of elements. The repetition should be perceptible and systematic. Apply to tiles, panels, screens, or repeated structural elements. Do not use for irregular or random patterning. This tag is about regular repetition, not complexity.
examples_positive: A wall covered in identical tiles arranged in a grid.
A facade with evenly spaced vertical fins.
examples_negative: A wall with randomly sized tiles.
A plain surface with no pattern.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect repeated shapes or textures with consistent spacing and scale. Use pattern matching or autocorrelation to confirm regularity. Ensure the repetition spans a meaningful area. Avoid tagging when repetition is only a small detail.
extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface tiling or repeated geometry modules. Compute periodicity and uniform spacing across surfaces. Tag when repetition is regular and covers a significant portion of the view.

definition_short: Seating is present and available for use within the space.
definition_long: Use when chairs, benches, stools, or other seating objects are visible and appear intended for use. This tag is about availability, not density or comfort. Apply to public or private spaces with any clear seating provision. Do not use when no seating objects are visible. If seating is purely decorative and not usable, do not apply.
examples_positive: A lobby with chairs arranged near the entrance.
A corridor with a bench along the wall.
examples_negative: An empty hallway with no chairs or benches.
A room with only standing counters.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect seating objects such as chairs, benches, or stools. Confirm that the objects are placed in usable positions rather than stacked or stored. A single seat can qualify if clearly visible. Avoid counting seats that are too distant to confirm.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object class labels to identify seating meshes. Tag when at least one seating object is visible in the primary field of view. Optionally check accessibility to ensure seats are not blocked.

definition_short: A prominent central feature anchors the space and draws visual attention.
definition_long: Use when a distinct object or architectural element sits near the center of the space and serves as a focal point. The landmark should be visually salient by size, contrast, or uniqueness. Apply to sculptures, reception desks, atrium features, or central installations. Do not use for peripheral features or subtle decor. This tag emphasizes centrality and salience.
examples_positive: A large sculpture in the middle of a lobby.
A central reception desk anchoring a hall.
examples_negative: Small artwork on a side wall.
Multiple small objects with no clear center.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for a salient object near the visual center with strong contrast or scale. Use saliency maps and spatial position to confirm central placement. The landmark should dominate composition more than surrounding elements. Avoid tagging if the focal point is off to the side.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute object saliency and distance to spatial center of the room. Tag when a central object has high visual exposure along typical paths. Use visibility and scale metrics to confirm dominance.

definition_short: Horizontal lines, layers, or bands dominate the visual composition of the space.
definition_long: Use when horizontal elements such as long bands, slabs, or layered lines are the primary visual cue. The emphasis is on horizontal directionality and alignment. Apply to spaces with strong horizontal datum lines, low-slung volumes, or repeated horizontal bands. Do not use when vertical or diagonal elements dominate. This tag is about directional emphasis, not symmetry.
examples_positive: A lobby with long horizontal wood slats along the walls.
A facade with continuous horizontal bands and ledges.
examples_negative: A space dominated by vertical columns.
A room with diagonal or irregular geometry.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect line orientations and verify a majority of strong edges are near horizontal. Look for repeated horizontal bands across large areas. Avoid tagging if horizontality appears only in minor details. Use orientation histograms to quantify dominance.
extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface edges and structural lines for orientation distribution. Tag when horizontal edges significantly exceed vertical or diagonal counts. Use geometry normals and edge direction statistics.

definition_short: HVAC vents, grilles, or diffusers are visible and identifiable in the scene.
definition_long: Use when mechanical air vents or grilles are clearly visible on ceilings, walls, or floors. The vents should be identifiable as HVAC elements rather than generic patterns. Apply to spaces where such fixtures are part of the visual field. Do not use when vents are hidden or too small to confirm. This tag is about visibility of mechanical ventilation.
examples_positive: A ceiling with multiple rectangular air diffusers.
A wall with a large ventilation grille.
examples_negative: A ceiling with only recessed lights and no vents.
A space where vents are hidden behind panels.
extraction_notes_2d: Use object detection for vent or grille shapes and check context near ceilings or walls. Rectangular or circular diffusers with slats are typical cues. Confirm scale and placement consistent with HVAC. Avoid false positives on speakers or lighting.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object metadata for mechanical fixtures and mesh labels. Tag when HVAC outlet objects are visible in the view. Consider normal orientation and placement on ceilings or walls to validate.

definition_short: The space appears maintained and in good repair, with minimal visible wear or damage.
definition_long: Use when surfaces, fixtures, and furnishings show little to no visible damage, stains, or deterioration. This is about condition and upkeep rather than cleanliness alone. Apply when the overall impression is cared-for and functional. Do not use if there are notable cracks, peeling paint, or broken fixtures. This tag can co-occur with high_cleanliness but is distinct.
examples_positive: A hallway with intact finishes and no visible damage.
A stairwell with clean, unbroken handrails and walls.
examples_negative: A room with peeling paint and broken tiles.
A space with damaged furniture and worn surfaces.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for cues of wear such as chipped paint, cracks, stains, or missing components. A consistent, intact finish across surfaces indicates good maintenance. Distinguish from cleanliness by focusing on damage rather than dirt. Avoid tagging if only a small area is maintained but others are damaged.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material condition annotations or defect detection models to identify damage. Tag when defect density is low across visible surfaces. Consider both structural elements and furniture condition.

definition_short: Work surfaces such as desks, tables, or counters are visible and intended for tasks.
definition_long: Use when surfaces suitable for working, writing, or task activities are clearly visible. The surfaces should be functional and accessible, not just decorative consoles. Apply to offices, studios, labs, or work areas with desks or counters. Do not use when only small side tables or decorative shelves are present. This tag is about the presence of work-capable surfaces.
examples_positive: An office with desks and chairs arranged for work.
A workshop with long workbenches.
examples_negative: A lounge with only coffee tables.
A hallway with a narrow decorative console.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect desk or table objects and verify scale consistent with working surfaces. Work surfaces often have chairs, monitors, or task tools nearby. Confirm accessibility and placement for use. Avoid tagging from tiny tables used for decor.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories and dimensions to identify work surfaces. Tag when at least one desk or workbench is visible and accessible. Use proximity to task objects to reinforce classification.

definition_short: Water elements such as fountains, pools, or streams are visible as part of the environment.
definition_long: Use when a water feature is clearly present and intended as an architectural or landscape element. This includes fountains, reflecting pools, indoor water walls, or visible streams. The water should be a visible, designed feature, not incidental moisture. Apply to indoor or outdoor scenes where water is a focal or notable element. Do not use for rain, wet floors, or small containers.
examples_positive: A lobby with a central fountain and water basin.
A courtyard with a reflecting pool.
examples_negative: A wet floor after cleaning.
A small glass of water on a table.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for water surfaces with reflections, ripples, or visible basins. Identify fountain structures, jets, or water walls. Confirm that water is part of the built environment. Avoid false positives from glossy floors or mirrors.
extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph objects tagged as water or fountain features. Check material properties such as reflectance and animated surfaces. Tag when water objects occupy a meaningful visible area or are central features.

definition_short: Hard, reflective materials like polished stone, metal, or glass dominate visible surfaces.
definition_long: Use when large surfaces are both hard and reflective, producing clear specular reflections. The focus is on material reflectivity, not merely brightness. Apply to polished stone floors, glass walls, or metal panels that reflect light or surroundings. Do not use for matte hard materials or small reflective accents. This tag can co-occur with glare if reflections are intense.
examples_positive: A polished marble floor reflecting overhead lights.
A lobby with large glass walls and reflective stone.
examples_negative: A matte concrete floor with no reflections.
A room with only small mirrors.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect specular highlights and mirror-like reflections on large surfaces. Look for consistent reflections of lights or objects across floors or walls. Confirm that the material is hard and glossy. Avoid confusing reflective lighting with actual reflective material.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material properties for roughness and reflectance to identify reflective surfaces. Tag when a significant portion of visible area has low roughness and high specular response. Combine with surface hardness classification.

definition_short: Ceiling height is noticeably high relative to human scale.
definition_long: Use when the space has a high ceiling that contributes to a sense of vertical openness. This can include double-height rooms, atriums, or tall halls. The cue is height relative to occupants or doors. Apply even if other materials are not notable. Do not use for standard or low ceilings.
examples_positive: An atrium with a ceiling spanning multiple stories.
A gallery with tall ceilings above large walls.
examples_negative: A typical office with standard ceiling height.
A basement with low headroom.
extraction_notes_2d: Use doors, people, or furniture as scale references to estimate ceiling height. Look for long vertical spans and extended wall height. Avoid tagging when wide-angle distortion exaggerates height. Confirm that the height is a dominant impression.
extraction_notes_3d: Estimate ceiling height from geometry and compare to human scale models. Tag when the height exceeds a defined threshold for high ceilings. Consider multi-level visibility as an additional cue.

definition_short: Lighting creates strong contrast between bright and dark areas, producing dramatic illumination.
definition_long: Use when illumination is uneven with pronounced bright spots and deep shadows. The key is lighting contrast rather than surface color contrast. Apply to scenes with spotlights, dramatic directional lighting, or strong light shafts. Do not use for evenly lit spaces. This tag can co-occur with glare if highlights are intense.
examples_positive: A gallery with bright spotlights and dark surrounding areas.
A corridor with strong sunlight patches and deep shadows.
examples_negative: A room with evenly diffused lighting.
A space with uniform ambient light.
extraction_notes_2d: Measure luminance contrast across regions and detect sharp light-shadow boundaries. High contrast lighting shows a wide dynamic range with localized bright spots. Ensure contrast is driven by illumination, not surface color. Avoid tagging when contrast is minor or limited to small accents.
extraction_notes_3d: Use lighting simulation outputs to assess illuminance variance. Tag when the ratio of bright to dark areas exceeds a threshold. Identify point or directional light sources causing strong gradients.

definition_short: Colors appear vivid and intense, with high saturation across major surfaces or objects.
definition_long: Use when the scene has strongly saturated hues that dominate the visual impression. This is about saturation intensity rather than the number of colors. Apply when large areas or key elements are vividly colored. Do not use when colors are muted or pastel. This tag is the counterpart of low_saturation.
examples_positive: A room with bright red walls and vivid blue accents.
A retail display with highly saturated product colors.
examples_negative: A space with muted beige and grey tones.
A pastel interior with soft colors.
scope_includes: intense color accents over large areas
extraction_notes_2d: Compute saturation histograms and assess average saturation for dominant regions. High saturation should appear across significant areas, not just small accents. Check for lighting that artificially boosts saturation. Avoid tagging when saturation is localized to tiny objects.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material color values and lighting to compute chroma levels. Tag when average saturation of major surfaces exceeds a threshold. Consider color grading or render settings that enhance saturation.

definition_short: Decor and furnishings resemble a residential or domestic environment.
definition_long: Use when the space has cues typical of homes, such as cozy furniture, personal decor, rugs, and warm, informal styling. The overall impression should feel domestic rather than institutional or commercial. Apply to rooms styled like living rooms, kitchens, or bedrooms. Do not use for offices or retail spaces even if comfortable. This tag is about decor style, not occupancy.
examples_positive: A lounge with sofas, rugs, and framed family-style art.
A kitchen-like space with domestic cabinets and decor.
examples_negative: A corporate lobby with formal finishes.
A hospital waiting room with utilitarian furniture.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for residential cues such as soft seating, rugs, throw pillows, and personal decor. Warm lighting and informal arrangements support the tag. Avoid tagging when furnishings are standardized or commercial. Use context clues like kitchen cabinetry or living room layouts.
extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories for home furniture and decor items. Tag when domestic furniture types and styling dominate the view. Consider layout patterns typical of living or dining rooms.

definition_short: Windows occupy a large portion of visible wall area and are a dominant architectural feature.
definition_long: Use when glazing spans large areas, such as floor-to-ceiling windows or extensive window bands. The emphasis is on window area proportion rather than just presence of windows. Apply when windows are a primary visual element and allow significant daylight or views. Do not use for small or few windows. This tag can overlap with glass_prominent but focuses on window area.
examples_positive: A lobby with a floor-to-ceiling glass facade.
A room with continuous bands of large windows.
examples_negative: A space with a single small window.
A room with small punched windows.
extraction_notes_2d: Estimate window area as a proportion of visible wall surfaces. Large window area appears as dominant glazing spanning much of the wall height or width. Use frame detection and transparency cues to separate windows from walls. Avoid tagging if windows are small relative to the wall.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute visible window surface area relative to wall area from geometry. Tag when glazing ratio exceeds a defined threshold. Use material tags for glass and boundary frames to estimate coverage.

definition_short: The space appears disordered or visually messy, with items lacking clear organization.
definition_long: Use when objects are arranged irregularly, with cluttered layouts or inconsistent alignment. The cue is visual disorder rather than density alone. Apply when the space lacks clear organization or systematic placement. Do not use for eclectic but orderly arrangements. This tag contrasts with high_orderliness.
examples_positive: A room with furniture placed haphazardly and items scattered.
A shop with products piled without clear arrangement.
examples_negative: A space with neat rows of chairs.
A room with a consistent grid layout.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for irregular spacing and misaligned objects across the scene. Low orderliness shows weak alignment along common axes. Distinguish from high density by focusing on arrangement quality. Avoid tagging when disorder is limited to a small area.
extraction_notes_3d: Compute alignment metrics for objects and furniture clusters. Tag when alignment variance is high and layout lacks dominant axes. Use spatial entropy measures to assess organization.

definition_short: Colors appear muted or desaturated, with low chroma across the scene.
definition_long: Use when the palette is subdued with low color intensity, often dominated by greys, beiges, or muted tones. The cue is low saturation, not necessarily low brightness. Apply when most surfaces and objects appear desaturated. Do not use when vivid colors are present on large surfaces. This tag is the counterpart of high_saturation.
examples_positive: A minimalist space with grey and beige tones.
An office with muted wall colors and neutral furniture.
examples_negative: A room with bright red and blue walls.
A colorful market scene.
scope_excludes: highly saturated accents over large areas
extraction_notes_2d: Compute average saturation for dominant regions and confirm low chroma. Ensure that low saturation is not due to lighting or exposure artifacts. Look for widespread muted tones rather than small neutral objects. Avoid tagging when strong color accents dominate.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material color values to measure chroma across surfaces. Tag when saturation levels are low across major materials. Consider lighting color temperature effects that can reduce perceived saturation.

definition_short: Metal surfaces are a dominant visible material in the scene.
definition_long: Use when metal elements occupy a substantial portion of the view, such as metal panels, railings, or structural elements. The material should read clearly as metal through reflectance or texture cues. Apply when metal is a primary finish or structural feature, not just small accents. Do not use for minor metal hardware. This tag is about material prominence.
examples_positive: An interior with large brushed metal wall panels.
A space with exposed steel beams and metal railings.
examples_negative: A room with only small metal door handles.
A space where metal is barely visible.
scope_excludes: painted surfaces that are not metal
extraction_notes_2d: Look for metallic reflectance, specular highlights, and characteristic textures like brushed or polished metal. Confirm that metal covers significant area or is visually dominant. Avoid confusing grey paint with metal. Use contextual cues like exposed steel members.
extraction_notes_3d: Use material classifications to identify metal surfaces and compute their visible area. Tag when metal surfaces exceed a threshold of the visible field. Combine with reflectance properties to confirm metallic materials.

definition_short: The overall palette emphasizes natural, earthy hues such as browns, greens, and muted warm tones.
definition_long: Use when the scene is dominated by earth tones like brown, tan, olive, terracotta, or muted green. The palette should feel natural and organic rather than saturated or synthetic. Apply when these hues cover major surfaces or decor elements. Do not use for vivid or high-contrast palettes. This tag is about overall color impression.
examples_positive: A space with tan walls, wood finishes, and olive textiles.
A cafe with terracotta tiles and muted green accents.
examples_negative: A room dominated by bright primary colors.
A monochrome black and white space.
extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue distribution and check for dominance of browns, tans, muted greens, and warm earth tones. Ensure these hues cover large areas, not just small accents. Avoid tagging if bright or cool colors dominate. Use material cues like wood or stone to support the palette.
extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors across major surfaces and compare to an earth-tone palette range. Tag when earth-tone coverage exceeds a threshold. Consider lighting that may shift hue perception.

definition_short: Images or representations of nature are visible, such as landscapes, plants, or natural scenes.
definition_long: Use when the space includes artworks, prints, murals, or graphics depicting natural elements like forests, mountains, water, or plants. The nature imagery should be clearly recognizable and intentional. Apply to posters, murals, digital displays, or printed panels. Do not use for real plants or natural materials; use indoor_plants or natural materials tags instead. This tag is about depicted nature.
examples_positive: A wall mural showing a forest landscape.
Framed photos of mountains in a corridor.
examples_negative: Real potted plants in a room.
Abstract art with no clear natural subject.
extraction_notes_2d: Use image classification or OCR context to identify depicted natural scenes. Look for recognizable natural elements in artwork frames or panels. Distinguish between real plants and images of plants. Ensure the imagery is visible and intentional.
extraction_notes_3d: Identify art assets or texture maps that depict nature. Use object metadata for wall art or displays to detect nature themes. Tag when nature imagery is visible from main viewpoints.

definition_short: The layout is open with few full-height partitions, creating a continuous shared space.
definition_long: Use when the space is largely continuous, with minimal full-height walls separating functions. The area reads as one shared volume, even if zones are defined by furniture. Apply to open offices, lofts, or large rooms without enclosed rooms. Do not use for compartmentalized or cell-based layouts. This tag is about plan openness.
examples_positive: An office floor with desks in a single open area.
A loft with kitchen, dining, and living zones in one room.
examples_negative: A clinic with many small rooms off a corridor.
A house with separate enclosed rooms.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for long sightlines and absence of full-height walls separating areas. Furniture groupings may define zones but should not block visibility. Use wall detection to estimate partition density. Avoid tagging if rooms are clearly separated by walls and doors.
extraction_notes_3d: Analyze the navigable space as a single large cell with few partitions. Tag when the number of enclosed rooms is low relative to area. Use wall height data to differentiate full-height partitions from low dividers.

definition_short: Windows are open, allowing direct airflow or connection to the exterior.
definition_long: Use when window panels are visibly open, such as casement windows swung out or sliding windows retracted. The opening should be clear and not merely implied. Apply to scenes where open windows are visible and intentional. Do not use for closed windows or fixed glazing. This tag is about physical openness.
examples_positive: A room with casement windows swung outward.
A corridor with sliding windows open to the outside.
examples_negative: Closed windows with no visible opening.
A glass curtain wall without operable windows.
extraction_notes_2d: Look for window frames and panels that are visibly displaced from the closed position. Gaps with visible exterior air or angle of window sashes indicate openness. Confirm that the opening is a window, not a door. Avoid tagging when only blinds are open.
extraction_notes_3d: Use window object states or hinge angles to detect openness. Tag when operable windows have non-zero open angles or retracted panels. Consider airflow indicators like curtains moving only as secondary cues.

definition_short: Architectural forms are predominantly rectilinear, with orthogonal walls, edges, and planar geometry.
definition_long: Use when the space is defined by straight lines and right angles, with boxy or orthogonal geometry dominating. The overall impression should be rectilinear rather than curved or faceted. Apply to standard rooms, offices, or modern interiors with orthogonal planning. Do not use when curvilinear or complex geometry is prominent. This tag is about dominant form language.
examples_positive: A room with straight walls, flat ceiling, and right-angle corners.
An office with rectangular partitions and orthogonal layout.
examples_negative: A space with sweeping curved walls.
A hall with faceted, angled surfaces.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect dominant line orientations and confirm strong horizontal and vertical edges. Right-angle corners and rectangular openings are key cues. Ensure curvilinear elements are minimal or secondary. Avoid tagging when diagonal or curved features dominate the view.
extraction_notes_3d: Use surface normals and edge directions to assess orthogonality. Tag when most surfaces align with orthogonal axes. Compute angular variance and confirm a rectilinear dominant geometry.

definition_short: Few or no people are present, indicating low human occupancy at the moment.
definition_long: Use when the space appears empty or nearly empty of people. The key cue is low person count relative to the space size. Apply to scenes with zero or very few people visible. Do not use when many people are present, even if the space is large. This tag is about current occupancy, not capacity.
examples_positive: A large lobby with no visible people.
A corridor with only one person at a distance.
examples_negative: A market with many shoppers.
A waiting room filled with people.
extraction_notes_2d: Detect people and estimate count per area. Sparse occupancy shows zero or very low person density across the frame. Ensure people are not obscured by occlusions or framing. Avoid tagging when people are present but partially hidden in large numbers.
extraction_notes_3d: Use agent counts or person detections in the scene graph. Tag when the number of visible occupants is below a threshold for the space size. Consider line of sight to avoid missing occupants behind obstacles.
